coredata.dc:title: Stable dispersed N-doped carbon quantum dots-decorated 2D Ni-BDC nanocomposites towards the tribological application
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Huawen
Liu, Zan
Cheng, Zhi-Lin
coredata.dc:description: The interfacial compatibility of nanomaterials was critical for enhancing dispersibility and tribological properties in oil. In this paper, the 2D Ni-BDC nanosheets were decorated with N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CDs) by in-situ solvothermal method, forming N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC nanocomposites constituted of ca. 9-layer nanosheet and 3 nm-size N-CDs nanoparticle. The composition and structure of N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC were revealed by a series of characterization techniques. The experiment results verified that the dispersibility of Ni-BDC/2D Ni-BDC nanocomposites was significantly improved compared with neat 2D Ni-BDC in the castor oil. In addition, we intensively investigated the tribological performance of N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC nanocomposites in the castor oil under different friction pairs. The results demonstrated that the average friction coefficient and wear scar diameter of the castor oil with 0.04 wt% N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC were reduced by 35.29% and 25% compared with 2D Ni-BDC under the ball-on-ball contact, and the average friction coefficient and wear rate were decreased by 20% and 12.3% under the ball-on-slider contact, respectively. The above results proved that the N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC nanocomposites were capable to effectively enhance the dispersibility and tribological performance in castor oil.
coredata.dcterms:subject: 2D Ni-BDC
N-CDs
Modification
Tribological performance
Castor oil

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst pubtype ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of N-CDs and N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC nanocomposites
Preparation of 2D Ni-BDC and N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC-based oils

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a, b) XRD and FT-IR spectra of N-CDs, 2D Ni-BDC and N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC; (c) PL spectra of N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC and 2D Ni-BDC at the excitation wavelength of 280 nm and fluorescence photos of N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC before and after excitation light irradiation in ethanol solution (inset); (d) PL spectra of N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC and N-CDs (inset) at the excitation wavelength of 260–340 nm and 240–440 nm, respectively.
(a) TEM image and particle size distribution diagram (inset) of N-CDs; (b) TEM images of 2D Ni-BDC; (c) SEM images of N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC nanocomposites; (d) TEM images of N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC nanocomposites.

(a, b) HRTEM image, FTT (inset) and corresponding SAED pattern of N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC nanocomposites; (c) HRTEM image of N-CDs on the surface of the N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC nanocomposites; (d-h) Elemental mapping analysis of N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC nanocomposites.
(a) XPS spectra of 2D Ni-BDC and N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC; (b-d) Ni 2p, C 1 s, O 1 s and N 1 s deconvoluted spectra of N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC nanocomposites and 2D Ni-BDC.

(a, b) the particle size distribution of 2D Ni-BDC and N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC nanocomposites after ultrasound in castor oil and standing for 0d (D0) and 12d (D12); (c) Variation of relative mass concentration of N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC and 2D Ni-BDC along with settling time; (d, e) Contact angles of castor oil droplets on 2D Ni-BDC and N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC sheets.

(a, b) Variation curve of friction coefficient with time, average friction coefficient and wear diameter of castor oil, 2D Ni-BDC (0.04 wt%) - castor oil and N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC (0.04 wt%) - castor oil under ball-on-ball contact; (c, d) Variation curve of friction coefficient with sliding time, average friction coefficient and wear rate under ball-on-slider contact of castor oil, 2D Ni-BDC (0.04 wt%) - castor oil and N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC (0.04 wt%) - castor oil.
Raman spectrum and 3D laser scanning micrographs (inset) of the worn surfaces lubricated by N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC-castor oil on ball-on-ball contact (a) and ball-on-slider contact (b).
Curve-fitted XPS spectra of the worn surface lubricated by 0.04 wt% N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC-castor oil: (a) Ni 2p, (b) C 1 s, (c) O 1 s, (d) N 1 s and (e) Fe 2p.

head.ce:title: Stable dispersed N-doped carbon quantum dots-decorated 2D Ni-BDC nanocomposites towards the tribological application

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou 225002, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou 225002, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The construction and performance of Ni-BDC/2D Ni-BDC nanocomposites.

The interfacial compatibility of nanomaterials was critical for enhancing dispersibility and tribological properties in oil. In this paper, the 2D Ni-BDC nanosheets were decorated with N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CDs) by in-situ solvothermal method, forming N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC nanocomposites constituted of ca. 9-layer nanosheet and 3 nm-size N-CDs nanoparticle. The composition and structure of N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC were revealed by a series of characterization techniques. The experiment results verified that the dispersibility of Ni-BDC/2D Ni-BDC nanocomposites was significantly improved compared with neat 2D Ni-BDC in the castor oil. In addition, we intensively investigated the tribological performance of N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC nanocomposites in the castor oil under different friction pairs. The results demonstrated that the average friction coefficient and wear scar diameter of the castor oil with 0.04 wt% N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC were reduced by 35.29% and 25% compared with 2D Ni-BDC under the ball-on-ball contact, and the average friction coefficient and wear rate were decreased by 20% and 12.3% under the ball-on-slider contact, respectively. The above results proved that the N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC nanocomposites were capable to effectively enhance the dispersibility and tribological performance in castor oil.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, the prosperity of researches focusing considerable attentions in various two-dimensional (2D) layered materials has promoted a remarkable development in structural superlubricity, such as graphene and transition-metal dichalcogenides (TMDs) [1–3]. A large number of studies had proved that 2D nanomaterials could significantly improve the tribological properties of lubricants [4–6]. However, the interface issues of 2D inorganic nanomaterials have severely restricted their practical application, which became one of the most challenging efforts in past [7,8].

As an emerging lubricant additive, the metal-organic framework (MOF) have been proved to exhibit good wear resistance due to its flexible skeleton framework and great chemical stability [9]. Initially, Dong’s group deeply studied the tribological properties of 3D MOFs as lubricant additives [10–12], and found that they could effectively improve the load-bearing and anti-wear properties in lubricating oils; however, they failed to exert the friction reducing performance. Afterwards, our group intensively probed the dispersion stability and tribological properties of 2D MOFs in lubricants in recent years [13,14]. The results confirmed that 2D MOFs not only had the better dispersibility than inorganic nanomaterials in base oil, but also improved both the anti-wear and anti-friction performances, evidently superior to 3D MOFs to some extent. However, 2D MOFs in the base oil exhibited weak mechanical stability due to higher porosity, which was prone to collapse at the initial stage of friction resulted in the aggravation on the rubbed surface [15]. For this reason, some pioneer works conducted to functionalize the surface of MOFs with other materials as the matrix of the package frame, which based on the existence of coordinated unsaturated metal sites and tunable organic linkers on the surface of MOFs with large pores. For example, Niu et al. [16] prepared the SIB@ZIF-8 (SIB: 29%) with encapsulating the organic sulfide SIB in the framework of ZIF-8, which reduced by 40% wear volume in base oil. Wu et al. [17]constructed the dialkyldithiophosphate (DDP) functionalized Zr-MOFs (UiO-66 @ DDP和UiO-67 @ DDP) nanoparticles by ultrasonication assisted self-assembly method, which reduced by 43.6% and 51.3% friction coefficients in the base oil, respectively. Therefore, the MOFs composites containing the functional matters with the anti-friction properties exerted the complementary role of each material for the sake of the mechanical stability of MOFs. However, the above-mentioned functionalized materials contained the sulfur element that failed to meet the requirements of environmental protection, thus severely hindering their further application [18,19]. Therefore, it was an urgent to discover the environmentally friendly anti-friction nanomaterials with the better biocompatibility for the improvement of the tribological properties of 2D MOFs as additive in oil. Among multitudinous 2D MOFs catalogs, 2D Ni-BDC nanomaterials as a promising alternative candidate, were of the appropriative advantages of large specific surface area, robust stability and many exposed active sites, which has been used as a tough scaffold for nanoparticles to form hybrid material possessing many exclusive functions [20]. Moreover, nickel atom was apt to deposit on the surface of sliding steel to form a stable and continuous protective film, acting as an anti-seize agent [21]. Thus, the low-cost 2D Ni-BDC was a prospective objective adopted as 2D MOFs lubricating material.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a new type of zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials, have been used as lubricant additives owing to their unique properties such as size effect, controllable functionalized surface, good biocompatibility and so forth [22]. Xiao et al. [23] found that the friction coefficient of deionized water with water-soluble CQDs was reduced by 30%. In recent years, by virtue of the surface microstructure of CQDs, more reports corroborated that the surface functionalization of CQDs materials could effectively enhance the affinity with the metal friction surface [24]. Ye et al. [25] reported that the N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CDs) had the better tribological performance than CQDs, and the average friction coefficient of PEG containing N-CDs in oil was decreased by 75%, much more improvement than that of PEG-CQDs. Alternatively, CQDs were yet employed in the fabrication of the friction-reducing composite materials to improve the dispersibility and tribological properties, which relied the “rolling effect” and “self-mending effects” of CQDs in the friction [26]. Shang et al. [27] fabricated the CQDs/rGO composites with the long-term dispersion for 6 months in PEG by employing one-step pyrolysis, which effectively reduced by 71.4% friction coefficient and 66.9% wear volume in PEG. Sarno et al. [28] prepared the polymethylmethacrylate/carbon quantum dots (PMMA/CQDs) via one-step method, which significantly improved the friction and wear properties. Consequently, the nanocomposites loaded with a large amount of CQDs exhibited the robust dispersion stability and synergistic lubrication effect. More importantly, CQDs were of the low toxicity and environmental friendliness, which should be taken into account to further improve the dispersion and tribological performance of 2D MOFs.

In this work, the as-obtained N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC nanocomposites were prepared by in-situ solvothermal method. The structure and composition of the N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC were extensively determined by the characterizations. The dispersion and stability of the N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC in castor oil were evaluated by dynamic light scattering (DLS) and ultraviolet visible spectrum (UV–Vis). Finally, the tribological behaviors of the N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC in castor oil were intensively investigated under ball-on-ball and ball-on-slider contacts, respectively, and the corresponding friction mechanism was also discussed.

Nickel acetate tetrahydrate (Ni(OAc)2·4H2O, ≥99.0%), 1,4-terephthalic acid (1,4-H2BDC, 99.0%), N-N dimethylacetamide (DMAC, ≥99.5%), glucose, urea (99.0%), castor oil (≥99.5%), ethanol (EtOH, ≥99.5%) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). Deionized water was made by Yangzhou University (Jiangsu, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of N-CDs and N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of the N-CDs consulted to the pioneer work [29]. Firstly, 0.6 g glucose and 0.6 g urea were mixed into 30 mL deionized water. Then, the mixture was transferred into a PTFE-lined stainless-steel autoclave, and heated at 200 ℃ for 10 h. After natural cooling to room temperature, the N-CDs aqueous solution was obtained by purification with the N-CDs aqueous solution was obtained by purification with 1000 Da molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) dialysis membrane for 24 h, followed by filtration through 0.22 μm microfiltration membrane. The physical photo of the resulting the N-CDs solution is showed in Fig. S1 (a) (supporting information).

The synthesis of the 2D Ni-BDC nanosheets referred to the reference [30], and the synthesis of the N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC was fulfilled by in-situ solvothermal method. Initially, 0.0496 g Ni(OAc)2·4H2O was dissolved in 12 mL deionized water, and 0.0166 g of 1,4-H2BDC involved with 1.5 mL of N-CDs were dissolved in 12 mL DMAC by stirring for 30 min. Then, the above resulting solutions mixed were transferred into a homogeneous reactor, and heated at 150℃ for 3 h. After cooling to room temperature, the precipitate was collected by suction filtration and washed alternately with water and ethanol. Finally, the N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC composite was obtained after dried at 60 ℃ for 12 h, as showed in Fig. S1 (b) (supporting information). For the sake of testifying the repeatability, three batches of samples were repeatedly synthesized under the above same conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of 2D Ni-BDC and N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC-based oils
ce:section.ce:para: In order to compare the dispersion and friction properties of N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC and 2D Ni-BDC in castor oil, 0.04 g N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC or 2D Ni-BDC was dispersed into 10 g castor oil by grinding, and then performed by ultrasonic treatment, obtaining the suspension, denoted N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC-castor oil or 2D Ni-BDC-castor oil, as showed in Fig. S1 (c) (supporting information).

The tribological testing were recorded on a four-ball friction machine (MMW-1, Jinan Chenda Ltd Co., Jinan City, China) and reciprocating tester (MFT-5000, Rtec-Instruments, Silicon Valley, California, USA), respectively. The four-ball testing was performed on the ball-on-ball contact by rotating an AISI GGr15 steel ball (12.7 mm in diameter; initial surface roughness of 0.08 μm, Fig. S1 (a), supporting information) over three AISI GGr15 steel balls under the following testing conditions: rotating rate of 1200 rpm, duration of 6 h, load of 100 N, and the maximum non-seizure load (PB) was tested by four-ball friction machine. The ball-on-slider contact was implemented on the multi-functional reciprocating tester under the following testing conditions (load of 10 N; frequency of 4 Hz; distance of 2 mm; duration time of 30 min; initial surface roughness of slider with 0.005 μm, Fig. S1 (b), supporting information). Each testing repeated at least three times to take the average value. After the ending, we used an optical microscope to measure the wear scar diameter and the 3D micro-morphology equipped in MFT-5000 was used to measure the macroscopic surface topography of worn surface, including wear trace and depth after testing.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded on D8 advance powder X-ray diffractometer (Bruker AXS, Germany). The fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) were recorded on TENSOR27 FTIR spectrometer (Bruker Optics, Germany). The fluorescence spectra (PL) were recorded on the F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (HITACHI, Japan). The transmission spectra (TEM) were obtained by JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). The Ultraviolet visible absorption spectra (UV–vis) were recorded on the Thremo-1780 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (TMO, USA). Dynamic light scattering measurements (DLS) were obtained by Mastersizer 3000 ultrahigh speed intelligent particle size analyzer (Malvern, UK). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was recorded on ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo, USA). The Raman spectra were acquired on a Labram-1B (Dilor) confocal microscopy Raman spectrometer with 532 nm wavelength incident laser light. The wear scar micrographs were observed by an LSM 700 3D laser scanning microscope. HR-TEM images, element mappings and selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern were obtained by the Tecnai G2 F30 S-TWIN field emission transmission electron microscope (FEI, USA).

Fig. 1 shows the XRD, FT-IR and PL spectra of N-CDs, 2D Ni-BDC nanosheets and N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC nanocomposites. As shown in Fig. 1 (a), the obtained N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC nanocomposites have a similar crystal phase with the well-defined Ni-based MOF ([Ni3(OH)2-(1,4-BDC)2(H2O)4]·2H2O, CCDC No. 638866). Besides, the diffraction peaks of the N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC at 2θ = 8.9°, 11.5°, 15.3°, 18.1°, 23.5°, 28.4° and 31.2° completely match with those of the 2D Ni-BDC and have no appearance of the typical peaks of the N-CDs, indicating that the crystal structure of 2D Ni-BDC is well preserved in N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC and the existence of N-CDs with definitely broadened peaks cannot affect the growth of 2D Ni-BDC [31,32]. As shown in Fig. 1 (b), for the 2D Ni-BDC, the absorption peaks at 765 cm−1 and 813 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of Ni and the in-plane bending vibration of Ni-O [31], and the characteristic peaks at 1377 cm−1 and 1568 cm−1 are separately associated to the stretching vibrations of the O = C-O group and the C = C bond of the benzene ring, and the peak at 3487 cm−1 is corresponded to the stretching vibration of –OH [33]. In addition, the broad absorption peaks of the N-CDs between 2970 cm−1 and 3718 cm−1 belong to the stretching vibration of –NH and –OH, and the peaks at 1402 cm−1 and 1633 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibrations of C-N and C = O, respectively. More importantly, besides the remaining characteristic peaks of the 2D Ni-BDC and N-CDs, the spectra of N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC emerge some exclusive feature changes. The typical peaks of –NH and –OH attached to the N-CDs disappear in the spectrum of the N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC, while the stretching vibration peak at 3458 cm−1 attributed to –OH of N-CDs appears the red-shift approaching to 3427 cm−1 of the N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC. These changes indicate that the formation of the hydrogen bonds between the N-CDs and 2D Ni-BDC is achieved by the interface interaction of the surface active groups in the growth period of the 2D Ni-BDC [34]. Fig. 1 (c, d) describes the fluorescence emission spectra (PL) of the N-CDs, 2D Ni-BDC nanosheets and N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC nanocomposites. Distinctly, the N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC exhibits the stronger fluorescence intensity than 2D Ni-BDC at the excitation wavelength of 280 nm, which shows the blue fluorescence when dissolved in ethanol (inset). According to Fig. 1 (d), the Stokes shift distance of the N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC is reduced by ca.10 nm, and the optimal emission wavelength is also blue-shifted from 470 nm of the N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC to 370 nm of the N-CDs. This phenomenon could be attributed to the formation of hydrogen bond between the amino group on the surface of N-CDs and the carboxyl group of 2D Ni-BDC, and the amino coordination causes steric hindrance resulting in the weakening of the conjugate system [35]. Furthermore, on account of the decease of the amino-functional groups of the N-CDs surface in the N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC involved with the surface-active groups of the 2D Ni-BDC [36], which enables the N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC to exhibit the excitation-independent characteristic. The above results further illustrate that the 2D Ni-BDC and N-CDs in N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC are connected by chemical bonding and exert a synergistic effect.

Fig. 2 displays the SEM and TEM images of the N-CDs and N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC nanocomposites. Definitely, the as-prepared N-CDs particles have the spherical structure with uniform 3 nm size (Fig. 2 (a)). As shown in Fig. 2 (b, c), the N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC nanocomposites throughout keep the layered nanosheet structure with a thickness of about 8.89 nm in spite of the introduction of N-CDs, which are in good agreement with 2D Ni-BDC [30]. Nonetheless, the ongoing magnification TEM image (inset) can clearly recognize the massive well-dispersed nanoparticles with the identical size of N-CDs evenly scattered on the surface of nanosheets (Fig. 2 (d)). The HRTEM image (Fig. 3 (a)) of the N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC reveals the crystal lattice fringes assigned to (100) plane of 2D Ni-BDC with the lattice spacing of 0.98 nm, which is consistent to FFT (the fast fourier transform) pattern showing the dominantly exposed (100) plane (inset: Fig. 3 (a)). In addition, the corresponding SAED pattern (Fig. 3 (b)) shows the diffraction spots along [100]-zone axis corresponding to the diffraction facet of (100), indicating that the 2D Ni-BDC nanosheets in nanocomposite have the better crystallinity and the highly exposed (100) crystal facet illustrates the restricted growth of MOFs along the vertical direction [37]. Hence, the as-prepared N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC nanocomposites are identified to 2D ultrathin nanosheets corresponding to about nine-layered structure stacking along a axis by calculating in terms of the thickness and the lattice spacing. The HRTEM image of N-CDs (Fig. 3 (c)) coated on the surface of 2D Ni-BDC nanosheets shows the lattice spacing of 0.23 nm, which is compatible to the reference [29]. More importantly, the elemental mapping results (Fig. 3 (d-h)) display that the N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC nanocomposite is composed of the evenly distributed C, O, N and Ni elements. The emergence of the N element derived from the N-CDs indicates that the N-CDs are successfully decorated onto the surface of the 2D Ni-BDC. These results suggest that the effectively embedding introduction of N-CDs on the surface of 2D Ni-BDC is conductive to affect the lattice and the surface structure of 2D Ni-BDC, which is likely to become a fantastic role in improving the friction properties [38]. For the sake of testifying the reproducibility of the interface, the PL and FT-IR characterization results shows that the three batches of samples are uniform, convincing the better interface reproducibility (Fig. S3, supporting information).

Fig. 4 shows the XPS spectra of 2D Ni-BDC and N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC nanocomposites. The XPS survey spectrum of the N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC contains the main peaks of C 1 s, Ni 2p, O 1 s and N 1 s (inset), in which the C 1 s, Ni 2p and O 1 s peaks are assigned to 2D Ni-BDC, and the N 1 s peak belongs to N-CDs. In addition, the peaks of the Ni 2p spectrum at 855.1 and 872.3ev (Fig. 4 (b)) are attributed to 2p 3/2 and 2p1/2 of Ni2+, respectively. The high-resolution C 1 s spectrum of the N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC is decomposed into three peaks at 285.0, 285.6 and 288.8 eV, which are assigned to the C-C/C = C, C-O/C-N and O-C = O, respectively (Fig. 4 (c)). More importantly, the high-resolution N 1 s spectrum of N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC appears C-N bond (399.5 eV) corresponding to the 285.6 eV peak of deconvoluted C 1 s spectrum, but the 2D Ni-BDC spectrum cannot observe it. This result is identical to the FT-IR spectra of the N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC, which is ascribed to the near disappearance of the amino group originated from the N-CDs [25]. The above results further demonstrate that the N-CDs are evenly anchored on the surface of 2D Ni-BDC nanosheets by chemical bonding, which is conducive to improve the dispersion stability and tribological properties of 2D MOF in oil.

As the lubricant additive, the dispersibility of nanomaterials in oil is one of the crucial factors to achieve excellent lubrication performance [39]. Fig. 5 shows the dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurement and photographs (inset) of 0.04 wt% 2D Ni-BDC and N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC nanocomposites suspension after ultrasonic treatment and thereafter standing for 12 days in castor oil. The laser particle size analysis shows that the average particle size of the initial N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC locates at 1380 nm, whereas that of the initial 2D Ni-BDC situates at 2032 nm from the Fig. 5 (a, b), indicating that the N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC is easier dispersion than the individual 2D Ni-BDC nanosheets. Fantastically, the particle size distribution peaks of N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC and 2D Ni-BDC after standing for 12 days move forward to 899 nm and 955 nm, respectively. This suggests that the N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC is of oil-soluble feature. Furthermore, the as-obtained N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC nanocomposites in castor oil shows better transparency degree than that of 2D Ni-BDC (inset). More importantly, the variation diagram of the mass concentration of N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC and 2D Ni-BDC along with settling time is accessed by the UV–Vis spectrum (Fig. 5C and Fig. S4, supporting information). After standing for 12d, the relative mass concentrations of the N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC and 2D Ni-BDC have 74% and 52% of the initial mass concentration yet, respectively. The above results indicate that the dispersibility of the 2D Ni-BDC in castor oil is greatly enhanced through N-CDs surface modification. The reason is ascribed to two aspects. On the one hand, the interface affinity of the N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC is superior to the 2D Ni-BDC by the discrepancy of the contact angles of the castor oil droplet on the 2D Ni-BDC and N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC sheets (Fig. 5 (d, e)); on the other hand, the oxygen-containing functional groups in N-CDs (such as carboxyl, hydroxyl, carbonyl and epoxy) can interact with the polar groups in castor oil to enhance the dispersibility of the N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC in polar castor oil [40].

Fig. 6 describes the tribological performance of 0.04 wt% N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC nanocomposite and 2D Ni-BDC as lubricant additives in castor oil by using the ball-on-ball contact and ball-on-slider contact, respectively. The survey of adding content finds that the optimal adding content appears at 0.04 wt% both 2D Ni-BDC and N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC in two contacts as shown in Fig. S5 (Supporting information), Fig. S6 (a) (Supporting information) and Fig. S7 (a) (Supporting information). As shown in Fig. 6 (a, b), the average friction coefficients of the N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC and 2D Ni-BDC with 0.04 wt% adding content in castor oil are evidently lower than that of castor oil. More importantly, the former exhibits the lower friction coefficient (COF) than the latter throughout the friction time. The average COF and AWSD of the N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC-castor oil are decreased by about 35.29% and 25.00% compared with 2D Ni-BDC-castor oil, and approximately 66.67% and 30.64% lower than castor oil. Furthermore, the maximum non-seizure load (PB) of the N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC-castor oil is dramatically improved by 30.22% compared with the 2D Ni-BDC-castor oil (Fig. S6 (c), supporting information). In order to intuitively assess the wear surface, the macroscopic surface topography of the worn ball surface of steel balls is observed by 3D micro-morphology (Fig. S8, supporting information). The wear surface of the N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC-castor oil shows both the smaller and smoother scar and the shallower scratch than those of the pure castor oil and 2D Ni-BDC that display the deeper score and more furrows. Consistent with the microstructure of the worn surface, the N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC-castor oil has the smallest surface roughness (Ra = 0.09 μm) (Table S1, supporting information). On the contrary, the surface roughness of castor oil and Ni-BDC-castor oil are accessible to 0.19 μm and 0.16 μm, respectively, while they show a harsh wear surface. These above results demonstrate that the tribological performance of 2D Ni-BDC in castor oil is effectively improved by the ball-on-ball contact with modification of the N-CDs. Similarly, 0.04 wt% N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC in castor oil shows the preferable friction performance under the ball-on-slider contact with a load of 10 N (Fig. S7 (b), supporting information). As showed in Fig. 6 (c, d), the friction coefficient curve of the N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC-castor oil depending on the friction time shows distinctly lower and more stable than those of another two lubricant, which can lesson 20% COF and 12.3% wear rate compared with 2D Ni-BDC-castor oil. The running-in period at the initial stage in the process of sliding friction can determine the evolution of wear behavior to steady state [41]. The shorter running-in period of the N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC-castor oil indicates the exceptional dispersion and stability ability of the N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC in oil. Specifically, the N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC-castor oil also shows shallower and smoother wear marks and optimum surface roughness (N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC-castor oil:0.377 μm,2D Ni-BDC-castor oil: 0.456 μm, castor oil: 0.79 μm) (Fig. S9 and Table S1, supporting information). These results confirm that the N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC can more promote the anti-wear performance of castor oil than 2D Ni-BDC.

In order to explore the defect effect of the N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC in the friction process, the tribological performance of the N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC depending on the additive content and loads are intensively investigated. Relative to the ball-on-slider contact, the effects of the additive content and loads to the N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC-castor oil are more apparent, so we select the ball-on-ball contact to probe the above effects. As shown in Fig. S6 (a) (supporting information), when the adding concentration of the N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC is up to 0.04 wt%, the friction coefficient and wear scar diameter are the lowest (COF: 0.066, WSD: 0.747 mm). As the further increasing of adding content, the friction coefficient and wear scar adversely increase more obviously. This is attributed to the formation of protective film depending on the adding content [42]. The excessive adding content resulting in the agglomeration and deposition on the friction surface is prone to entangle with the mechanical exfoliation to generate second damage of the wear surface [25,43]. Besides, the higher load also affects the friction coefficient and wear performance of N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC-castor oil. The wear scar diameter and roughness of N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC-castor oil are increased by 34.34% and 68.97% at 250 N load compared with 100 N, respectively (Fig. S6 (b) and Table 2, supporting information). The overload can cause the serious damage of the protective film formed by the N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC in the friction process, thereupon resulting in the loss of the ability to prevent the direct contact of the friction pairs [44]. Furthermore, the generation of friction heat increases significantly during the friction process as a result that the friction contact area becomes soft, leading to the further expansion of micro-cracks made of the wider and rougher wear surface that account for the increase of friction coefficient and wear scar diameter [45].

The 3D laser scanning micrographs, Raman and XPS spectra are used to characterize the worn surface to further probe the friction mechanism of the N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC in castor oil. According to the Raman spectrum of worn surface on ball-on-ball contact (Fig. 7 (a)), the characteristic peaks at 386 cm−1 and 680 cm−1 belong to iron oxide peak, indicating the steel ball surface is oxidized during friction and formed an iron oxide protective film [46]. Interestingly, the emergence of the peaks at 1355 cm−1 and 1589 cm−1 that are attributed to D-band and G-band of carbon characteristic bands, respectively, suggests the formation of carbon-containing iron oxide protective film on the worn surface. However, the Raman spectrum of worn surface on ball-on-slider contact only appears one peak of iron oxide (690 cm−1), indicating that the friction surface only exists the iron oxide protective film due to the lower load [47]. The XPS analysis is used to further detect chemical composition of worn surface lubricated by N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC-castor oil. As shown in Fig. 8 , there appear four peaks of Fe 2p, C 1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s in the XPS spectrum. The Fe 2p XPS spectrum (Fig. 8 (e)) is deconvoluted into three peaks at 706.9 eV, 710.9 eV and 724.4 eV [48], respectively. The peak at 706.9 eV is attributed to Fe atoms, and the peaks at 710.9 eV and 724.4 eV are attached to Fe3/2 and Fe1/2. Incorporated with the analysis result of the spectrum of O 1 s attributed to iron oxide (529.53 eV), a series of iron oxides form on the worn surface, such as FeO, Fe2O3 and Fe3O4 [49]. Alternatively, the Ni 2p, C 1 s and N 1 s spectra are in good agreement with the XPS results of the N-CDs /2D Ni-BDC. These indicate that the continuous deposition gives rise to the formation of the trio-film containing iron oxides, inorganic carbon and nitrogen, which can effectively reduce the friction coefficient and wear scar diameter [26,50].

Herein, we successfully synthesized the N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC nanocomposites via in-situ solvothermal method. The characterization results illustrated that the interface interaction was achieved between the amino groups on the surface of N-CDs and the carboxyl groups on the 2D Ni-BDC nanosheets, thereby resulting in the N-CD uniformly loaded on the surface of 2D Ni-BDC. The N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC nanocomposite exhibited excellent dispersibility in castor oil for 12 days without any obvious precipitation, and the relative mass concentration of N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC-castor oil still retained at 74% of the initial mass concentration. More importantly, the tribological behavior of N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC nanocomposite in castor oil showed apparent decline in the friction coefficient and wear scar diameter or wear rate, and obvious augment in carrying capacity both the ball-on-ball contact and ball-on slider contact. Therefore, N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC could be applied as the friction reducing additive to effectively improve the tribological properties of lubricants, which was prospective to the feasible application of 2D MOF as additive in lubricants.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2022.152428.

ce:section.ce:para:
